title: Faulty Rotated Binary Search: Index Out of Bounds

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: You will be given an array of integers A and a target integer `x`. The array A has been rotated `k` times.
statement.outputFormat: Return the index of `x` in A if it exists, otherwise return -1.
statement.notes: The array contains distinct integers, and `x` is present in A.

examples.explanation: First line: Number of elements, second line: A, third line: x.

hints.text: Try to identify the correct pivot index for the Binary Search.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The correct implementation of Binary Search should be based on the rotation count.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute Force algorithm has a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.optimalSolution: We can solve this problem using a modified Binary Search algorithm that accounts for the rotation count.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of our algorithm can be proved by using the standard Binary Search proof techniques.
commonMistakes: Failure to account for rotation count

functionSignature.cpp: int search(vector<int>& A, int x, int k, int n);
functionSignature.java: int search(int[] A, int x, int k, int n);
functionSignature.python: def search(self, nums: List[int], x: int, k: int, n: int) -> int:
functionSignature.javascript: var search = function(A, x, k, n);
